Undid revision [[Special:Diff/1274745251|1274745251]] by [[Special:Contributions/39.124.92.9|39.124.92.9]] ([[User talk:39.124.92.9|talk]]) It does. See abstract "The application of the masking tec]hnique to produce precise surface patterns of both n- and p-type is described" p- and n- are the two types of field effect transistors. silicon dioxide is the metal oxide (MOS) in this case.
Misrepresentation of sources, which never claim the 1957 device is a MOSFET or field-effect transistor. MOSFET wasn't developed until 1959.

Suggestions for simplicity, avoiding redundant linking
Terms such as "prototype" don't need quotes  (except, ironically, when being cited like this).

Suggestions for simplicity, style. (Proportional fonts have embedded kerning tables for optimal spacing, so manual double-spacing of sentences isn't necessary, and can even lead to obsessive behaviour—that is, besides the obsessive behaviour of describing why sentences needn't be manually double-spaced, which I have obsessively done here.)

In English, a contraction is defined as a shortening of a word; “transresitance

Undid revision 1097289128 by [[Special:Contributions/Hadamardsense|Hadamardsense]] ([[User talk:Hadamardsense|talk]]). There is no indication the editor conducted the source to see what it says about the term. Also, normally a new term comes from existing terms; although transresistance could have been defined as a property of a circuit before the invention of the transistor, there was no such device as a transresistor.

/* Bipolar transistors */  In the History section, it originally stated that the name “transistor” was a contraction of “transreistance.” While the article The History of the Transistor has a more complete discussion, one of the relevant properties of a transistor is its transresistance, but the device itself is a transresistor, much as resistance is a relevant property of a device named “resistor.”
